all.title: Online Calibration of LLM Evaluations via State-Novelty-Aware Bias Correction
all.rationale: Multiple branches (A, D, F, G, H) rely on zero-shot LLM action evaluations without adaptation, leading to brittleness when domain knowledge is lacking. The frontier adds memory but does not correct inherent LLM biases. Future work should learn a bias correction function conditioned on state novelty and trajectory statistics, updated online without fine-tuning the entire LLM. This targets a structural limitation: the assumption that pre-trained LLM evaluations are universally reliable.
all.meta_gap: Pre-trained LLM zero-shot evaluations are used without any mechanism to adapt to task-specific distribution shifts or systematic biases.
all.shared_mechanism: All these branches assume the LLM can provide useful action evaluations without task-specific adaptation, relying solely on pre-training.
all.non_obvious_property: Property: The bias correction must be a function of both state features and trajectory statistics, learned via a meta-learner that avoids interference with the LLM's pre-trained knowledge. Obvious composition that fails: Fine-tuning the LLM on task data (e.g., RLHF) fails because it requires many trial-and-error interactions and can overwrite general knowledge. Prior work 'Self-Consistency' (Wang et al., 2022) merely averages samples and does not correct systematic bias. Why prior work has not formalized this property: The community has focused on in-context learning and fine-tuning, not on online calibration of evaluation biases without weight updates.

all.title: Metacognitive Budgeting for Memory-Augmented Monte Carlo Tree Search
all.rationale: All recent evolution actions (branches A-F) replace prior approaches with memory-augmented MCTS, converging on this paradigm. However, computational complexity remains a bottleneck. Future work should introduce a metacognitive controller that allocates search budget per node based on expected uncertainty reduction from memory access, effectively pruning the tree where memory provides reliable evaluations. This addresses the scalability issue that persists even after memory augmentation.
all.trend: Replacement of static/sequential planning with memory-augmented MCTS
all.trend_endpoint: Universal adoption of memory-augmented tree search for LLM-based planning
all.implied_next_problem: The high computational cost of MCTS, even with memory, limits scaling to large branching factors or long horizons.
all.non_obvious_property: Property: The budget allocation must be proportional to the derivative of evaluation uncertainty with respect to computational cost, forming a gradient-based resource allocation. Obvious composition that fails: Using a learned heuristic (e.g., PUCT) for pruning fails because it does not consider the memory's own uncertainty estimates. Prior work on reducing MCTS cost (e.g., 'RAVE' Gelly & Silver, 2007) relies on domain-specific patterns and does not integrate memory uncertainty.

all.title: Dynamic Action Discovery through Grammar-Constrained Generation with Environment Validation
all.rationale: Branches C and E assume a fixed, known set of valid actions, limiting applicability to environments with dynamic action spaces. Future work should enable the agent to generate new actions using the LLM, constrained by a learnable grammar that captures environment-specific syntax and semantics, and validated through partial execution. This would break the assumption of static action sets.
all.meta_gap: The method assumes a fixed set of valid actions, precluding scenarios where actions can be discovered or composed.
all.shared_mechanism: Both branches assume the environment provides a static enumeration of valid actions, and the agent simply selects from this list.
all.non_obvious_property: Property: The discovered actions must satisfy a cross-validated consistency between the LLM's natural language description and the environment's actual effect, learned through a contrastive objective that enforces both syntactic validity and semantic plausibility. Obvious composition that fails: Using LLM to freely generate actions (as in 'SayCan' Ahn et al., 2022) fails because it ignores environment-specific syntax and may produce invalid actions. Prior work on action generation does not enforce dynamic grammar learned from feedback.
